items.text:
• Case 1:
• Input: n, (v0, ..., vn−1)
• Output: (w0, ..., wn−1) such that (w0, ..., wn−1) is a permutation of
(v0, ..., vn−1) and w0 ≤ ... ≤ wn−1
• Case 2:
• Input: n, (R0, ..., Rn−1) with the keys k0, ..., kn−1
• Output: (R (cid:48)
0, ..., R (cid:48)
n−1) such that (R (cid:48)
0.k0 ≤ ... ≤ R (cid:48)

(R0, ..., Rn−1) and R (cid:48)

• Data structure
Array a[0..n − 1]
a[0] = v0, ..., a[n − 1] = vn−1

• Basic principle:
• (i, j) with i < j is an inversion if a[i] > a[j]
• While there is an inversion (i, i + 1) interchange a[i] with a[i + 1]
• Algorithm:
Procedure bubbleSort(a, n)
begin
last ← n − 1
while (last > 0) do
n1 ← last–1; last ← 0
for i ← 0 to n1 do
if (a[i] > a[i + 1]) then
swap(a[i], a[i + 1])
last ← i
end

• Analysis
• Worst-case performance
a[0] > a[1] > ... > a[n − 1]
Time for searching: O(n − 1 + n − 2 + ... + 1) = O(n2)
TbubbleSort(n) = O(n2)
• Best-case performance: O(n)

• Basic principle:
suppose a[0..i − 1] sorted
insert a[i] such that a[0..i] becomes sorted
• Algorithm (search sequentially the position of a[i]):
Procedure insertSort(a, n)
begin
for i ← 1 to n − 1 do
j ← i–1 // a[0..i − 1] sorted
temp ← a[i] // search the place of temp
while ((j ≥ 0) and (a[j] > temp)) do
a[j + 1] ← a[j]
j ← j–1
if (a[j + 1]! = temp) then
a[j + 1] ← temp
end

• Example
3 7 2 1
3 7 2 1
2 3 7 1
1 2 3 7
• Analysis
• searching the position i in a[0..j − 1] needs O(j − 1) steps
• worst-case scenario a[0] > a[1] > ... > a[n − 1]
Search time: O(1 + 2 + ... + n − 1) = O(n2)
TinsertSort(n) = O(n2)
• best-case scenario: O(n)

• Apply the following scheme:
• the current step: select an element and bring it on the final position in
the sorted table;
• repeat the current step until all elements reach the final positions.
• After the type of the selection of an element:
• Naive selection: choose elements in the order they are initially (from
n − 1 to 0 or from 0 to n − 1)
• Systematic selection: use max-heap

• In the order n − 1, n − 2, ..., 1, 0, namely:
(∀i)0 ≤ i < n =⇒ a[i] = max{a[0], ..., a[i]}
Procedure naivSort(a, n)
begin
for i ← n − 1 downto 1 do
imax ← i
for j ← i − 1 downto 0 do
if (a[j] > a[imax]) then
imax ← j
if (i! = imax) then
swap(a[i], a[imax])
end
• The time complexity for all cases is O(n2)

• organize the table like a
max-heap: (∀k)1 ≤ k ≤
n − 1 =⇒ a[k] ≤ a[(k − 1)/2];
• initially the table satisfies the
max-heap property starting with
the position n/2;
• insert in max-heap the elements
from the positions
n/2 − 1, n/2 − 2, · · · , 1, 0.
Phase II

• select the maximum element
and bring it on his place by
interchanging it with the last
element;
• decrease n by 1 and then
redo the max-heap;
• repeat the above steps until
all elements reach their
place.

content_blocks.title: The operation of insertion in heap

items.text:
Procedure insertTth(a, n, t)
begin
j ← t
heap ← false
while ((2 ∗ j + 1 < n) and not heap) do
k ← 2 ∗ j + 1
if ((k < n − 1) and (a[k] < a[k + 1])) then

k ← k + 1
if (a[j] < a[k]) then
swap(a[j], a[k])
j ← k
else
heap ← true
end

Procedure heapSort(a, n)
begin
// build the max-heap
for t ← (n − 1)/2 downto 0 do
insertTth(a, n, t)
// remove
r ← n − 1
while (r > 0) do
swap(a[0], a[r ])
insertTth(a, r , 0)
r ← r − 1
end

• building the heap (suppose n = 2k − 1)
i=0 2(k − i − 1)2i = 2k+1 − 2(k + 1)
(cid:80)k−1
• remove from heap and redo the heap
i=0 2i2i = (k − 2)2k+1 + 4
(cid:80)k−1
• complexity of the sorting algorithm
TheapSort(n) = 2nlogn − 2n = O(nlogn)

• P(n): problem of dimension n
• base:
• if n ≤ n0 then solve P by elementary methods
• divide-et-impera:
• divide P in a problems P1(n1), ..., Pa(na) cu ni ≤ n/b, b > 1
• solve P1(n1), ..., Pa(na) in the same way and obtain the solutions
S1, ..., Sa
• assemble S1, ..., Sa to obtain the solution S to the problem P

Procedure DivideEtImpera(P, n, S)
begin
if (n ≤ n0) then
find S by elementary methods
else
Divide P in P1, ..., Pa
DivideEtImpera(P1, n1, S1)
...
DivideEtImpera(Pa, na, Sa)
Assemble(S1, ..., Sa, S)
end

• generalization: a[p..q]
• base: p ≥ q
• divide-et-impera
• divide: m = [(p + q)/2]
• subproblems: a[p..m], a[m + 1..q]
• assembling: interclass the sorted subsequences a[p..m] ¸si a[m + 1..q]
• initially store the result of interclassing in temp
• copy from temp[0..q − p + 1] in a[p..q]
• complexity:
• time: T (n) = O(n log n)
• additional space: O(n)

• the problem:
• given a[0] ≤ a[1] ≤ · · · ≤ a[m − 1], b[0] ≤ b[1] ≤ · · · ≤ b[n − 1],
construct c[0] ≤ c[1] ≤ · · · ≤ c[m + n − 1] such that
(∀k)((∃i)c[k] = a[i]) ∨ (∃j)c[k] = b[j]) and for k! = p, c[k] and c[p]
come from different elements
• the solution
• initially: i ← 0, j ← 0, k ← 0
• the current step:
• if a[i] ≤ b[j] then c[k] ← a[i], i ← i + 1
• if a[i] > b[j] then c[k] ← b[j], j ← j + 1
• k ← k + 1
• termination criterion: i > m − 1 or j > n − 1
• if it is the case, copy in c the elements from the unfinished table

• generalization: a[p..q]
• base: p ≥ q
• divide-et-impera
• divide: find k between p and q by interchanges such that after finding
k we have:
• p ≤ i ≤ k =⇒ a[i] ≤ a[k]
• k < j ≤ q =⇒ a[k] ≤ a[j]
• subproblems: a[p..k − 1], a[k + 1..q]
• assembling: none

• initially:
• x ← a[p] (may choose x arbitrarily from a[p..q])
• i ← p + 1; j ← q
• the current step:
• if a[i] ≤ x then i ← i + 1
• if a[j] ≥ x then j ← j − 1
• if a[i] > x > a[j] si i < j then
swap(a[i], a[j])
i ← i + 1
j ← j − 1
• termination:
• the condition i > j
• operations
k ← i − 1
swap(a[p], a[k])

Procedure partitioning(a, p, q, k)
begin
x ← a[p]
i ← p + 1
j ← q
while (i <= j) do
if (a[i] <= x) then
i ← i + 1
if (a[j] >= x) then
j ← j − 1
if (i < j) and (a[i] > x) and (x > a[j])

then
swap(a[i], a[j])
i ← i + 1
j ← j − 1
k ← i − 1
a[p] ← a[k]
a[k] ← x
end

Procedure quickSort(a, p, q)
begin
while (p < q) do
partitioning (a, p, q, k)
quickSort(a, p, k − 1)
quickSort(a, k + 1, q)
end

• The choice of the pivot influences the efficiency of the algorithm
• Worst-case scenario: the pivot is the smallest (the biggest) value.
The time is proportional with n + n − 1 + ... + 1.
• TquickSort(n) = O(n2)
• The recursion tree:

• A ”good” pivot divides the table in two subtables of comparable
dimensions
• The height of the recursion tree is O(log n)
• The average complexity is O(n log n)

• Assumption: a[i] ∈ {1, 2, . . . , k}
• Find the position of each element in the sorted table by counting how
many elements are lower than him
1 Procedure countingSort(a, b, n, k)
2 begin
3
for i ← 1 to k do
c[i] ← 0
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
c[a[j]] ← c[a[j]] + 1
for i ← 2 to k do
c[i] ← c[i] + c[i − 1]
for j ← n − 1 downto 0 do
b[c[a[j]] − 1] ← a[j]
c[a[j]] ← c[a[j]] − 1
4
5
6
7
8
9
10
11
12 end
Complexity: O(k + n)

• Algorithm:
Procedure bucketSort(a, n)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
insert(B[(cid:98)n · a[i](cid:99)], a[i])
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
sort the list B[i]
concatenate in order the lists B[0], B[1], · · · , B[n − 1]
end
The average complexity: O(n)
(Cormen T.H. et al., Introducere ˆın algoritmi)

content_blocks.title: When a sorting algorithm is favourite?

items.text:
• A sorting method is stable if it keeps unchanged the relative order of
elements with identical keys
• Recommendations
• Quick sort: when you don’t need a stable method and the average
performance is more important than the worst one; O(n log n) - the
average time complexity, O(log n) auxiliary space
• Merge sort: when a stable method is required; time complexity
O(n log n); drawback: O(n) auxiliary space, a larger constant than
QuickSort
• Heap sort: when you don’t need a stable method and you are
interested more in the worst case performance than the average one;
time O(n log n), space O(1)
• Insert sort: when n is small

content_blocks.title: When a sorting algorithm is favourite?

items.text:
• Under some limited conditions, it is possible a sorting in O(n)
• Counting sort: the values are in an interval
• Bucket sort: the values are approximately uniformly distributed
